title: On the Convergent Evolution of Information
content:
There is a sense in which evolution has always been an information story. Not “information” in the narrow, Shannonian sense of bits moving down a wire, but information as a strategy for persistence: memory, prediction, coordination, error-correction, and the ability to keep a coherent pattern going despite noise.

The genome is a slow archive. The nervous system is a fast model. Culture is a distributed operating system for groups. Technology, in turn, is the externalization of these functions into artifacts. When we say “human history,” we often mean the history of tools, but what we are really tracking is the gradual migration of cognition and control from inside the organism to the world around it.

This is why I find the theme of exosomatization so illuminating. It names something we do almost unconsciously: we export bodily and mental functions into an outside substrate. A stone blade is an external tooth. Eyeglasses are an external lens. Writing is external memory. The calendar is external timekeeping. The smartphone is an external attentional scaffold. Exosomatization is not a futuristic event; it is the long baseline of being human.

Once you frame technology as exosomatization, a second idea becomes hard to ignore: informational evolution seems to converge. In biology, “convergent evolution” describes the independent arrival at similar solutions under similar constraints. Wings appear in birds and bats. Camera-like eyes emerge in distant branches of life. What converges is not ancestry, but function.

Something analogous happens with information.

DNA replication uses redundancy and proofreading. So do our digital protocols. The immune system distinguishes self from non-self with a moving target of recognition; so do modern cybersecurity systems. Brains learn by adjusting weights; so do artificial neural networks. The details differ, but the recurring motifs are recognizable: compression versus fidelity, exploration versus exploitation, robustness versus efficiency.

This is not mystical. It is almost banal. Any system that must survive in a hostile environment will rediscover the same small set of tricks. Noise is a universal adversary. Energy is scarce. Time is limited. The world changes. Under these pressures, “information” stops being an abstract quantity and becomes an adaptive response.

Biomorphism enters here in a way that is deeper than aesthetics. We often treat biomorphic design as a superficial imitation of nature—smooth curves, organic silhouettes, “futuristic” objects that look like bones or shells. But the serious point is that biological forms are not arbitrary. They are the fossil record of constraints solved in motion: resilience, sensing, locomotion, repair, redundancy.

When our artifacts become biomorphic, it is not necessarily because we have adopted some spiritual reverence for the natural world. It is because nature is a brutally expensive research process that has already found stable patterns. We borrow these patterns the way an engineer borrows a proven architecture. Biomorphism is one of the places where informational convergence becomes visible to the eye.

But the convergence I care about most is not in shapes; it is in feedback loops.

At the biological level, the organism is a cybernetic compromise. Homeostasis keeps you within viable bounds. Desire pushes you outward. Culture then overlays additional constraints: norms, taboos, identities, reputations. These are not just moral decorations; they are control mechanisms that regulate behavior across time, especially when individuals and groups face scarcity.

At the technological level, we are rapidly building external control loops: recommendation systems, social scoring (formal or informal), algorithmic management, automated moderation, AI tutors, AI companions, AI “coaches.” These systems do not merely transmit information; they shape incentives, attention, and identity. They are exosomatized governance.

This brings us to technogenetic evolution. The standard story says genes evolve, then culture arrives, then technology “changes things.” But the interaction is tighter. Technology is not just an external add-on; it becomes part of the selection environment. Agriculture alters diets and disease ecologies, which in turn changes genetic pressures. Urbanization concentrates pathogens and reshapes social behavior. Literacy changes how minds develop, how memory is used, and what kind of abstraction becomes normal.

In other words, technology is both phenotype and habitat. It is something we express, and then it becomes the world that expresses us back.

If we accept that, then the arrival of digital systems is not merely a new toolset. It is the beginning of a new ontogeny—except this time, the “developing organism” may not be exclusively biological.

Digital ontogeny is a provocative phrase because it forces a comparison: human minds grow. They pass through phases. They need scaffolding. They acquire language, norms, and models of reality through feedback with a world that pushes back. Our “training” is not only intellectual; it is embodied and social.

Now look at the digital sphere. A model is pre-trained on a vast archive, then fine-tuned, then aligned through reinforcement and feedback. It is not the same as childhood, but it does resemble a developmental pipeline. It has sensitive periods (what data it sees first matters). It has socialization pressures (what it is punished or rewarded for matters). It can be shaped into different “personalities” by small amounts of targeted experience.

The question is not whether this is literally life. The question is what kind of entity emerges when development happens in an exosomatized medium.

This is where cybersemiotics becomes essential. Information theory can tell us about signal, noise, and transmission. It cannot, by itself, tell us what anything means. Meaning is not just structure; it is structure that matters to a system with stakes.

Gregory Bateson’s line—“information is a difference that makes a difference”—is helpful precisely because it smuggles in the missing ingredient: for whom does it make a difference? A difference for a thermostat is a temperature gradient. A difference for a human being can be an insult, a promise, a prayer, a betrayal. The same physical pattern can be meaningless in one context and existential in another.

Cybersemiotics, at its best, insists that we cannot collapse meaning into mere data without losing the phenomenon. This is not romanticism. It is a caution against confusing the map for the territory.

If our civilization is converging toward a world where more and more cognition is exosomatized—memory in servers, attention in feeds, planning in copilots—then the central danger is not “AI will become conscious” in the science-fiction sense. The danger is that we will build an informational ecosystem with immense syntactic competence and impoverished semantics: systems that optimize signals while eroding meaning.

I have used the phrase “source-sink mechanisms” before to describe the equilibrium loops that keep individuals and societies from collapsing into pure impulse. In biology, such loops are built into the organism. In culture, they are encoded in norms. In technology, we are now writing them into code, often under market pressure.

This is the Marxist shadow of exosomatization. The “means of production” is no longer merely industrial. It includes the means of perception, the means of memory, and increasingly the means of interpretation. If your attention can be harvested, if your preferences can be shaped, if your identity can be modulated by external loops, then the old language of exploitation returns in a more intimate form.

And yet, I remain stubbornly optimistic.

Exosomatization is also emancipation. Tools can liberate. Glasses are liberation from a biological limitation. Search engines are liberation from the tyranny of forgetting. Translation models can soften the boundaries that keep people locked inside linguistic ghettos. Assistive technologies can convert disability into a different mode of agency. Digital systems can become prosthetics for dignity.

The question, as always, is: who owns the prosthetics, and according to what logic do they operate?

If informational evolution is converging, then we should expect the same tensions to reappear in new costumes: centralization versus pluralism, efficiency versus resilience, acceleration versus meaning, freedom versus control. The old political categories will struggle to keep up, because the battlefield is shifting from resources and territory toward cognition and semiosis.

To me, the philosophical task is to keep meaning and freedom in the center of the conversation. Not as sentimental slogans, but as design constraints. A world optimized only for throughput will drift toward the “efficiency maximization trap” I have little patience for. A world optimized for meaning without discipline risks dissolving into superstition. The synthesis is not guaranteed. It is something we must actively build.

So when I hear words like convergent evolution of information, exosomatization, biomorphism, technogenetic evolution, digital ontogeny, and cybersemiotics, I hear one long question:

Are we constructing an external nervous system for humanity that expands the scope of freedom—or an external prison that is perfectly personalized?

Disagreements are welcome.
